provenance.title: Differential Smoothing Mitigates Sharpening and Improves LLM Reasoning
provenance.abstract: The paper addresses diversity collapse in RL fine-tuning of LLMs, proving formally why it occurs (a selection and reinforcement bias), and proposes differential smoothing, a reward-shaping method that provably improves both correctness and diversity, outperforming vanilla RL and entropy-based heuristics.
key_claims: RL fine-tuning of LLMs (e.g. GRPO) exhibits diversity collapse ('sharpening') due to a formally provable selection and reinforcement bias
Differential smoothing (reward-shaping using the base model's own log-probability) provably improves both correctness (Pass@1) and diversity (Pass@K) simultaneously
DS-GRPO outperforms vanilla GRPO, entropy bonus/penalty heuristics, GR-PKPO, Unlikeliness Reward, and CISPO baselines
On Countdown, DS-GRPO improves Pass@K by ~4% across all K and yields a 4x inference speedup (Pass@64 of vanilla matched at k=16)
On math reasoning (5 benchmarks, 3 model sizes 1.5B-8B), DS-GRPO improves Pass@1 by 0.2-2.9% and Pass@64/AIME24 by up to 6.7%

architecture.primary_variant: DS-GRPO (Differential Smoothing applied to GRPO) on Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct (Countdown task)

modules.name: policy_model

parameters.base_checkpoint: Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct (Countdown) / Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B, Qwen3-1.7B, Qwen2.5-Math-7B, Mistral-8B-Instruct (math reasoning)

modules.notes: No custom architecture — reuses off-the-shelf pretrained/instruct-tuned LLMs unchanged; the paper's contribution is entirely in the RL objective/reward-shaping, not the network architecture.

modules.name: reference_base_model
modules.output_shape: [B, T, V] logits (log-probabilities used for KL and reward shaping)

parameters.role: provides pi_base(tau) used in KL term and in the differential smoothing reward

modules.notes: Ambiguity: whether pi_theta_old or pi_base is used in practice is only justified post-hoc in Appendix B.3 — flagged as an ambiguity below.

modules.name: grpo_advantage_estimator
modules.notes: Standard GRPO group-standardized advantage: (r_i - mean)/std over G sampled rollouts per prompt.

modules.name: differential_smoothing_reward_shaper

parameters.gamma_p: penalizes correct trajectories by base log-prob
parameters.gamma_n: boosts incorrect trajectories by base log-prob

modules.notes: This is the paper's central architectural contribution: a piecewise reward/advantage modification applied differently depending on whether the trajectory is correct (r>0) or not (r<=0).

differences.description: only the positive-trajectory (gamma_p) modification is applied; negative trajectories unmodified
only the negative-trajectory (gamma_n) modification is applied; positive trajectories unmodified
baseline with no differential smoothing (gamma_p = gamma_n = 0)

mathematical_spec.name: Solution Multiplicity
undefined_symbols: A(x) — number of distinct correct solutions for problem x, defined qualitatively but not with an explicit formula in the extracted text

datasets.name: Countdown (synthetic arithmetic puzzle: reach target from 3-4 integers in [1,99], each used once)
Knight and Knaves / Countdown-3 (Solution Multiplicity analysis)

special_conditions: High clip ratio 0.25 / Low clip ratio 0.20 (asymmetric PPO-style clipping)
Trained 'until reward saturates' rather than a fixed step count

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Optimizer is AdamW with a small constant or lightly-warmed-up learning rate typical of RLHF/GRPO fine-tuning (commonly ~1e-6 to 5e-6 for LLM policy fine-tuning)
implementation_assumptions.basis: literature default — not stated in paper; AdamW + small LR is near-universal for LLM RL fine-tuning (GRPO/PPO) in the DeepSeek-Math/DAPO lineage this paper builds on

implementation_assumptions.assumption: GRPO clip epsilon = 0.2 (standard PPO-style default), matching DAPO's asymmetric clipping described in the paper (high=0.25 / low=0.20 for math tasks)
implementation_assumptions.basis: paper explicitly gives high/low clip ratios for math tasks (0.25/0.20) but not for Countdown; assumed same values apply to Countdown by default
alternatives: different clip values specific to Countdown, unreported

implementation_assumptions.assumption: beta_KL (KL regularization coefficient) is a small positive value in the same range as forward-KL GRPO implementations (~0.001-0.04); paper studies sensitivity across a range but does not give a single default for main-text experiments
implementation_assumptions.basis: common practice + paper's own stability ablation implies beta_KL was varied but a canonical default must be assumed for the main reported numbers

implementation_assumptions.assumption: gamma_p=0.03 and gamma_n=0.01 (given explicitly for Countdown/Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) are assumed as the starting values to try first for a scaled-down reproduction, even on a different/smaller base model
implementation_assumptions.basis: explicitly stated in paper for the Countdown setup; math-task coefficient ranges were not given
alternatives: re-tune via small grid search if reproduction shows no effect

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Number of training steps/rollouts is determined empirically ('until reward saturates') rather than fixed; for a scaled-down free-Colab reproduction, a much smaller fixed step budget will be substituted to just demonstrate the sharpening-vs-smoothing effect qualitatively, not match paper's final absolute numbers
implementation_assumptions.basis: paper does not report exact steps; Colab compute budget forces a fixed, small step count regardless
alternatives: fixed step count determined by early stopping on a validation Pass@K plateau

ambiguities.description: Optimizer type, learning rate, number of training steps/epochs, warmup schedule, and gradient accumulation are not stated anywhere in the extracted text.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: AdamW, small constant LR (~1e-6-5e-6), trained until a small fixed step budget is exhausted (Colab-scoped)
alternatives: Different optimizer/LR values reported only in an appendix not captured by this extraction pass

ambiguities.location: Eq. 1 vs Eq. 6 — reference distribution used in practice
ambiguities.description: The theory uses pi_base in the KL term and reward shaping, but Appendix B.3 apparently justifies using pi_theta_old (the previous policy iterate) in practice instead of the frozen base model — the exact swap logic/justification was not captured in this extraction.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Use pi_theta_old (current policy's previous iterate) for the differential-smoothing log-prob term in the actual GRPO implementation, matching Eq. 6 notation
alternatives: Use the frozen pi_base throughout, matching Eq. 1/Eq. 4 notation literally

ambiguities.description: Eq. 2 references a per-problem quantity A(x) (apparently 'number of distinct correct solutions found') without an explicit closed-form definition being captured in this extraction pass.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: A(x) = count of distinct correct completions among K sampled rollouts for problem x
alternatives: A(x) could involve a semantic-equivalence dedup step not captured here

ambiguities.description: Exact dataset size and train/test split construction for the Countdown task were not stated in the extracted text.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: Use the standard, publicly available Countdown-RL dataset format (procedurally generated 3-4 integers in [1,99]) matching prior RL-reasoning papers this one builds on
alternatives: Paper may have used a custom-sized subset
